digest.top_line: SUPREMO: chest-wall RT halved 10yr recurrence (2.5%→1.1%, HR 0.45) with no DFS/OS gain; RNI-prohibited design can't license omitting nodal PMRT.
digest.tldr: Prostate carried the day's RT de-escalation signal. HYDRA's IPD meta-analysis: dose-escalated moderate hypofractionation adds late G2+ bowel toxicity (OR 1.48) for no PFS gain, reinforcing 60/20; an ASTRO SBRT review pins PACE-B's GU excess on an uncontoured urethra. In breast, SUPREMO's chest-wall RT benefit (HR 0.45) doesn't reach survival or license omitting nodal PMRT.

sites.intro: Two RT-technique reads converge on de-escalation: moderate hypofractionation at isodose (60/20) stays standard, and SBRT's GU toxicity tracks urethra dose constraints, not the modality.

studies.name: SBRT for Unfavorable Intermediate-Risk Prostate Cancer
studies.tldr: ASTRO 2024 educational round-up: 5-fraction SBRT matches conventional RT efficacy at ~1-2% bothersome toxicity; PACE-B's higher GU signal tied to an uncontoured urethra.
details: 🔍 ASTRO 2024 EDU 16 session (D. Spratt): role of SBRT in unfavorable intermediate-risk (UIR) prostate
🔍 UIR (a 2013 prognostic split) carries higher risk: GG3 HR 3.49, ≥2 intermediate-risk factors HR 2.40 for distant mets

details.text: 📊 RTOG 9408 secondary analysis (N=1,068): UIR prognosis and ADT benefit

details: 🔍 NCCN lists SBRT for ~all risk groups incl UIR, grounded in HYPO-RT-PC (N=1,200): 42.7Gy/7fx (6.1Gy) vs 78Gy/39fx
📊 PACE-B 5yr: no significant EFS difference (trend favoring SBRT)
📊 PACE-B G2+ GU 5.4% SBRT vs 3.7% conventional (p=0.28, ns); bowel toxicity not different
⚠️ Spratt critique: PACE-B urethra contoured only 'if visualized' → likely hotspots ≥48Gy (~121Gy EQD2), a plausible driver of the GU signal
📐 Dose↑ → better biochemical control (P=.018) but worse late G3+ GU (P=.014); Spratt attributes toxicity to missing dose constraints, not SBRT itself

details.text: 🔍 Three SBRT strategies discussed; field moving toward focal boost + whole-gland de-escalation
subdetails: Traditional: whole prostate to uniform dose
Micro-focal boost: full-dose gland + dominant-nodule boost
Focal boost + de-escalation: lower whole-gland dose, boosted nodule
Phase 1a/b: 36.25Gy prostate + nodule boost 45/47.5/50Gy, no G3+ toxicity
Loblaw: 35Gy prostate, 25Gy pelvis, 50Gy dominant intraprostatic lesion

studies.significance: SBRT's higher GU toxicity looks technique-driven, not intrinsic to ultra-hypofractionation: PACE-B constrained the urethra only 'if visualized', so hotspots likely reached ≥48Gy (~121Gy EQD2). The meta-analysis dose-response (biochemical control P=.018 but late G3+ GU P=.014) argues the fix is contouring and constraining the urethra, not backing off dose.
studies.monday_clinic: In unfavorable (not favorable) intermediate-risk localized prostate, RTOG 9408's secondary analysis supports adding short-course ADT to radiotherapy; it does not extend to favorable intermediate-risk, where ADT improved neither distant metastasis nor prostate-cancer mortality.
open_questions: Optimal SBRT approach: whole-gland vs focal boost vs whole-gland de-escalation
Role of urethral dose constraints in reducing SBRT GU toxicity
ADT addition to SBRT for unfavorable intermediate-risk

studies.name: HYDRA
studies.tldr: PFS non-inferior vs CFRT for isodose (HR 0.92) and dose-escalated (HR 0.94) MHFRT; dose escalation adds late G2+ bowel toxicity (OR 1.48).
details: 📊 Isodose vs dose-escalated MHFRT (each vs CFRT): efficacy + late toxicity — comparison values omitted (cell value "0.92" not verified in source)
🔍 IPD meta-analysis (MARCAP), 7 phase 3 RCTs: 3454 pts across 3 isodose-MHFRT trials, 2426 across 4 dose-escalated-MHFRT trials
🔍 Median f/u 5.4 yr (isodose), 7.1 yr (dose-escalated); CFRT comparator required ≥70 Gy in 2 Gy-equivalents
📐 Isodose MHFRT = same EQD2 as CFRT (eg 60 Gy/20 fx); dose-escalated MHFRT delivers a higher biological dose
📊 Both MHFRT schedules PFS-non-inferior to CFRT; dose escalation added no efficacy
⚠️ Dose escalation costs late bowel toxicity for nothing: G2+ GI OR 1.48 (p=0.0035), bowel-QoL decrement OR 1.68 (p=0.023), no PFS gain
⚠️ Isodose vs dose-escalated is an INDIRECT comparison (separate trial sets, separate CFRT controls), not a randomised head-to-head
🔗 Reinforces 60/20 (CHHiP/PROFIT-established) as standard MHFRT; argues against pushing to a higher-EQD2 hypofractionated schedule

verdict.rationale: IPD pooling of 7 phase 3 RCTs; both MHFRT schedules PFS-non-inferior. Reinforces moderate hypofractionation as standard and refines the regimen toward isodose.
verdict.audience: Localised prostate cancer, definitive external-beam RT

studies.significance: Dose escalation on top of hypofractionation buys no PFS (HR 0.94, ns) yet adds late G2+ GI (OR 1.48) and patient-reported bowel-QoL harm (OR 1.68). The regimen decision lands on isodose 60 Gy/20 fx, not a higher-EQD2 hypofractionated schedule.
studies.monday_clinic: In localised prostate cancer planned for moderate hypofractionation, this supports an isodose 60 Gy/20 fx schedule over dose-escalated MHFRT; it does not speak to ultrahypofractionated SBRT/5-fraction or the postoperative salvage setting.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: IPD meta-analysis (MARCAP consortium) of **7 phase 3 RCTs** of CFRT vs MHFRT, run as three separate meta-analyses: efficacy, physician-scored late toxicity, patient-reported QoL. Isodose pool **3454 pts (3 trials)**, dose-escalated pool **2426 pts (4 trials)**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Localised prostate cancer. Trials required modern-dose CFRT (**≥70 Gy in 2 Gy-equivalents**) plus published IPD on both efficacy and late toxicity; trials missing either were excluded.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: MHFRT split into **isodose** (same EQD2 as CFRT, eg **60 Gy/20 fx**) vs **dose-escalated** (higher biological dose). CFRT comparator ≥70 Gy EQD2.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: Primary efficacy: **progression-free survival**. Co-primary toxicity: late **G2+ GU and G2+ GI** (physician-scored). Co-primary PRO: clinically-significant **urinary and bowel QoL** decrement.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Both MHFRT schedules **PFS-non-inferior** to CFRT; the differentiator is toxicity (late GI and bowel QoL), see table.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Consistent with **CHHiP/PROFIT** establishing 60/20 non-inferiority; extends them by showing dose-escalated MHFRT trades added bowel toxicity for **no efficacy gain**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents localised prostate cancer treated with definitive external-beam RT. Does not represent ultrahypofractionated SBRT/5-fraction schedules or the postoperative salvage setting.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Isodose vs dose-escalated is an **indirect comparison** (separate trial sets, separate controls), not a randomised head-to-head. Physician-scored late toxicity harmonised across originally different scales.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The dose-escalated MHFRT bet (more biological dose in fewer fractions) does not pay off: no PFS gain, more late bowel toxicity. **Isodose 60/20 is the default MHFRT**; reserve dose escalation for a trial context.

open_questions: Isodose vs dose-escalated MHFRT lacks a randomised head-to-head
Durability of efficacy and late toxicity beyond 10 years

related_trials.brief_title: Testing Shorter Duration Radiation Therapy Versus the Usual Radiation Therapy in Patients With High Risk Prostate Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: This phase III trial compares stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT), (five treatments over two weeks using a higher dose per treatment) to usual radiation therapy (20 to 45 treatments over 4 to 9 weeks) for the treatment of high-risk prostate cancer. SBRT uses special equipment to position a patient and deliver radiation to tumors with high precision. This method may kill tumor cells with fewer doses over a shorter period of time. This trial is evaluating if shorter duration radiation…

related_trials.relevance_phrase: phase 3 SBRT vs 20-45fx RT, high-risk prostate

studies.name: SUPREMO
studies.tldr: Chest-wall RT cut 10yr chest-wall recurrence 2.5%→1.1% (HR 0.45) but no DFS/OS gain; RNI prohibited, so 'omit PMRT' overreads a chest-wall-only trial.

details.text: 📊 SUPREMO outcomes, chest-wall RT vs observation

details: 🔍 Randomized chest-wall irradiation alone vs observation after upfront mastectomy
🔍 Only 25% truly node-negative; majority had N1 (1-3 positive nodes)

details.text: 📐 RNI prohibited; nodal coverage in the PMRT arm was minimal

details: ⚠️ Not comprehensive PMRT: no axillary/SCV/IMN coverage, unlike Danish/BC, EORTC-22922, MA-20

details.text: 🔗 EBCTCG 2014 meta-analysis, PMRT benefit by nodal status
subdetails: 1-3 positive nodes: 10yr LRR cut 17.9%, 20yr breast-cancer mortality cut 8%

details: ⚠️ TNBC subgroup (only 10%) worse with PMRT on forest plot, likely underpowering not harm
🔗 Chinese TNBC RCT (n=681): chest-wall PMRT improved 5yr recurrence and OS, opposite here
⚠️ 65% HR+ but only 10yr follow-up; late recurrences likely underascertained

verdict.rationale: SUPREMO tested chest-wall RT alone (RNI prohibited, SCV in 12%, IMN <2%) in a mostly node-positive cohort; its null DFS/OS can't license omitting comprehensive PMRT.
verdict.audience: Post-mastectomy pN0 or 1-3 positive nodes, 65% HR+, 10% TNBC

studies.significance: The supplement, not the null headline, carries the RT read: even chest-wall-only RT cut node-positive LRR 4.8%→3.3% (HR 0.51, 0.27-0.96), nothing for node-negative. SUPREMO prohibited RNI and covered SCV in only 12%, so it can't license omitting comprehensive PMRT in 1-3 node-positive disease.
studies.monday_clinic: In post-mastectomy pts with 1-3 positive nodes, this argues against reading SUPREMO's null DFS/OS as license to skip comprehensive PMRT/RNI; it does not extend to truly node-negative pts, where no LRR benefit appeared.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Randomized chest-wall irradiation alone vs observation after upfront mastectomy; **RNI prohibited** in the RT arm. Non-UK centers could deliver RNI in the observation arm (12 control pts received supraclavicular RNI).

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Only **25%** were truly node-negative; the majority had **N1 (1-3 positive nodes)**. **65%** HR-positive, **10%** triple-negative. Reported **10-year** follow-up.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Chest-wall field only. Supraclavicular coverage in just **12%** of the PMRT arm (n=97), internal mammary in **<2%** across both arms. No comprehensive nodal (axillary/SCV/IMN) coverage.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Danish, British Columbia, EORTC-22922 (24% mastectomy) and MA-20 (lumpectomy) support comprehensive RNI in 1-3 node-positive disease. EBCTCG 2014: PMRT cut 10yr LRR **17.9%** and 20yr breast-cancer mortality **8%** in 1-3 node-positive women, none in node-negative.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents post-mastectomy pts with pN0 or 1-3 positive nodes, HR-positive-predominant, treated without RNI. Does not represent comprehensive PMRT (chest wall plus SCV/axillary/IMN), post-neoadjuvant patients, or ≥4 positive nodes.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Heterogeneous cohort (**25%** node-negative) may mask node-positive PMRT benefit, and the incomplete RT field cannot test comprehensive PMRT. TNBC subgroup (**10%**) is underpowered, and **10-year** follow-up is too short for HR-positive late recurrences.

open_questions: Does comprehensive PMRT with RNI benefit 1-3 node-positive disease with modern systemic therapy?
Does PMRT benefit differ by tumor biology (TNBC vs HR-positive)?

papers.title: When Postmastectomy Radiotherapy (PMRT) is not Really PMRT: A Critical Evaluation of SUPREMO Trial
papers.note: NOTE: add as a criticism of SUPREMO

papers.title: Hypofractionated radiotherapy for prostate cancer (HYDRA): an individual patient data meta-analysis of randomised trials in the MARCAP consortium.
papers.abstract:
BACKGROUND: Trials comparing moderately hypofractionated radiotherapy (MHFRT) to conventionally-fractionated radiotherapy (CFRT) for prostate cancer have varied considerably in intent (non-inferiority vs superiority) and MHFRT dose. We compare the efficacy and toxicity profiles of isodose MHFRT and dose-escalated MHFRT.

METHODS: This was an individual patient data meta-analysis that identified randomised phase 3 trials of CFRT versus MHFRT that had published individual patient-level data on efficacy and late toxicity. A systematic literature search using MEDLINE, Embase, trial registries, the Web of Science, Scopus, and relevant conference proceedings was initially conducted on Dec 15, 2023, and was re-conducted on Jan 8, 2025. Trials that did not publish efficacy data, did not publish late toxicity data, or did not use modern dose radiotherapy (≥70 Gy in 2 Gy equivalents) in the CFRT group were excluded. Individual patient data were provided to MARCAP by study investigators. Three separate meta-analyses were designed to compare efficacy (primary endpoint was progression-free survival), physician-scored late toxicity (co-primary endpoints were late grade 2 or higher genitourinary and late grade 2 or higher gastrointestinal toxic effects), and patient-reported outcomes (co-primary endpoints were clinically-significant decrements in patient-reported urinary or bowel quality of life) between patients receiving CFRT versus MHFRT.

FINDINGS: We identified 1696 records for review. Seven phase 3 trials comparing MHFRT with CFRT were eligible for inclusion in our analysis. Individual patient data were obtained from these seven studies (3454 patients from three trials comparing CFRT with isodose MHFRT and 2426 patients from four trials comparing CFRT with dose-escalated MHFRT). At a median follow-up of 5·4 years (IQR 4·6-7·2) for isodose MHFRT and 7·1 years (5·7-8·4) for dose-escalated MHFRT, no differences in progression-free survival were detected (hazard ratio 0·92, 95% CI 0·81-1·05; p=0·21 and 0·94, 0·82-1·09; p=0·43 respectively). No increased odds of grade 2 or higher genitourinary toxic effects were identified for either isodose (odds ratio [OR] 1·16, 95 CI% 0·86-1·57; p=0·32) or dose-escalated MHFRT (1·20, 0·95-1·51; p=0·13). The odds of grade 2 or higher gastrointestinal toxic effects were significantly higher with dose-escalated (OR 1·48, 95% CI 1·14-1·92; p=0·0035) but not isodose MHFRT (1·30, 0·59-2·87; p=0·51). Isodose MHFRT was not found to show different odds of urinary quality-of-life decrement (OR 1·03, 95% CI 0·51-2·09; p=0·93) or bowel quality-of-life decrement (0·76, 0·40-1·43; p=0·39). Dose-escalated MHFRT was associated with greater odds of bowel quality-of-life decrement (OR 1·68, 95% CI 1·07-2·61; p=0·023), but no evidence of greater urinary quality-of-life decrement was found (1·57, 0·87-2·85; p=0·13).

INTERPRETATION: Isodose MHFRT and dose-escalated MHFRT both have similar efficacy compared with CFRT, but dose-escalated MHFRT is associated with higher physician-scored and patient-reported bowel toxicity. Isodose regimens, eg, 60 Gy in 20 fractions, should be the standard MHFRT regimen for localised prostate cancer.

FUNDING: None.

papers.title: ASTRO 2024: SBRT for Unfavorable Intermediate-Risk Prostate Cancer
papers.abstract: ASTRO 2024, Prostate Cancer, stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT), external beam radiation therapy (EBRT), image-guided radiation therapy (IGRT), RTOG 9408 (NCT00002597) trial, PACE B trial, FLAME 2.0 trial, FORT trial.
